versionNotes: Sprint 4 W13 Gap Statement v2 — AI sparring on gap-the-gap and gap-vehicle, two questions marked optional, inspirational framing with worked examples
title: Gap Statement
description: The one sentence that shapes the next 2 weeks. Concrete gap = half-built defense. This is the most important thing you'll write all sprint.

questions.sectionTitle: Before you start — what strong gap statements sound like
questions.infoBlock:
Read this first · 60 seconds

Is this gap going to help you grow? If not, you’ll get feedback to sharpen it before Sprint 4 starts. A strong gap is specific, honest, and something you could actually work on in 2 weeks.

See what strong gap statements look like below:

Strong

“Goal-setting for ambiguity — I can set SMART goals when the problem is defined, but I freeze or default to busywork when the problem is fuzzy.”

Strong

“Giving hard feedback — I can receive and integrate critical feedback, but I soften my own feedback to peers past the point where it’s useful.”

Too vague

“Being a better learner” · “Getting better at Python” · “Improving my communication skills”
questions.prompt: Read the examples above, then start your draft below. You can always come back to these.

questions.infoBlock:
Field 1 of 5 · The gap

Name the SDL dimension AND the specific sub-skill you haven’t developed.

The dimension keeps you anchored in learning. The sub-skill keeps you honest about what specifically you haven’t done.
questions.prompt: One sentence naming your gap — dimension plus specific sub-skill.
questions.placeholder: Example: Metacognition — I can't notice when I've stopped learning and started performing.
questions.sparringRole: Is this a real gap, or a comfort zone the student is dressing up as a gap? Push gently: ask what's uncomfortable about naming this, or whether they've already half-solved it. Affirm real gaps that have specificity; question abstractions or skills-they-already-have.

questions.sectionTitle: The evidence this gap is real
questions.infoBlock:
Field 2 of 5 · Evidence

Two or three references from your retrospective that show this gap.

Where did you rate yourself “Weak evidence,” “No evidence,” or “Didn’t engage” that points at this gap?
questions.prompt: Two or three specific references from your retrospective that show this gap is real.
questions.placeholder:
Example:
1. In my retrospective I rated Metacognition as "No evidence" — I couldn't name a single Sprint 1-3 moment where I caught myself mid-shortcut.
2. My Sprint 2 Week 7 check-in: I wrote "everything is fine" when I'd actually skipped two group meetings. I didn't notice the gap between what I wrote and what I was doing.
3. My Sprint 3 reflection on the futures brief — I defended a claim I hadn't actually engaged with because I thought the instructor wanted me to have one.

questions.sectionTitle: Why this gap and not another
questions.infoBlock:
Field 3 of 5 · Why this one · Optional

One sentence. What makes this the one worth two weeks of your work?

If your answer is already implicit in Fields 1 and 2, you can skip this. Answer it only if naming the “why” sharpens your thinking.
questions.prompt: Optional — if it sharpens your thinking, one sentence on why this gap now.
questions.placeholder: Example: If I can't catch myself performing instead of learning, I'll keep doing it in my first job and never know why I'm stuck.

questions.infoBlock:
Field 4 of 5 · The vehicle

What concrete activity, project, or practice will you use as the container for closing this gap?

A technical skill (like learning a tool) can be the vehicle, but the gap itself has to be on a learning dimension, not the technical skill.
questions.prompt: What concrete activity, project, or practice will you use as the container for closing this gap?
questions.placeholder: Example: I'll use learning Next.js as the vehicle — but the work I'll document isn't "I shipped a Next.js app." It's a daily two-sentence journal where I catch myself either performing (copy-pasting from docs without understanding) or actually learning (writing something I could explain). The Next.js work is the excuse to generate those moments; the journal is the evidence of the gap closing.
questions.sparringRole: Can the student actually do this in 2 weeks? Ask: what's the first concrete thing you'd do on Monday? If the vehicle is vague or too ambitious, help them shrink it. If it's concrete and scoped, affirm it and ask what evidence they'll capture along the way.

questions.infoBlock:
Field 5 of 5 · What “closed” looks like

One sentence naming the observable evidence that would show the gap is smaller in two weeks.

NOT “I’ll feel better at it.” Something you could point at.
questions.prompt: One sentence naming observable evidence that the gap is smaller in two weeks.
questions.placeholder: Example: At least five journal entries where I caught myself performing in real-time and changed what I was doing in the next 10 minutes — with the before/after visible in the same entry.

questions.infoBlock:
Optional · For later

The clearest thing you’ll say about yourself in your defense likely starts with Field 1. Copy that sentence somewhere you’ll see it in Week 14 — a sticky note, a pinned message, wherever it lives for you. You’ll want to come back to it.
questions.prompt: Copy your Field 1 sentence somewhere you'll see it next week. Then export and submit.
